text: The surgeon divides the pleura and carefully mobilizes lymph nodes and lymphatics towards the esophagus. A lymph node is visibly being taken towards the specimen for removal.

The surgeon performs a complete mediastinal lymphadenectomy, dissecting infracarinal, right and left recurrent laryngeal nerve, and paratracheal lymph nodes. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided near the inferior pulmonary vein, with visible fat around the pericardium being taken towards the specimen. The pulmonary artery is visible as the assistant provides traction on the pericardium to separate fat, lymphatics, and lymph nodes from the heart and pericardium.

The surgeon dissects down to the right crest near the diaphragmatic hiatus, removing lymph nodes and fat near the pulmonary artery. The dissection progresses towards the subcarinal area, dividing both vagus nerves below the carina. The subcarinal lymph node dissection is visibly oozy due to vascularity, but bleeding is controlled with pressure or cauterization. The esophagus is dissected away from the left bronchus, encountering the first bronchial artery. The pleura is divided between the azygous vein and the posterior part of the esophagus, with caution taken to avoid injuring the thoracic duct visible in the fat between the azygous vein and aorta.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection, removing the primary tumor along with the pericardium, thoracic duct, azygous vein, intercostal vessels, bilateral pleura, and surrounding cuff of crura. Care is taken to avoid injuring the thoracic duct while ensuring all fat, lymphatics, and lymph nodes are taken towards the specimen.

The surgeon divides the left mediastinal pleura, taking it towards the specimen to achieve a proper margin, with the left lung visible through the surgical window. The dissection proceeds from the infra-azygous part to the supra-azygous part, where tracheal ring impressions are visible. The pleura near the azygous vein is divided, avoiding active energy sources near the tracheoesophageal membrane. Traction is applied to safely separate the pleura from the tracheal membrane. The arch of the azygous vein is freed, and two Chenamom hemlock clips are placed on either side before the vein is divided.

The surgeon dissects the area between the aorta and pulmonary vessel, moving towards the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and left paratracheal area. The assistant provides traction on the tracheal ring to facilitate dissection on both the ventral and dorsal sides of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. Sharp dissection is used to carefully remove a chain of lymph nodes present in this region.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the thoracic duct while dissecting a large lymphatic structure, minimizing bleeding by maintaining the proper anatomical plane. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, curving around the reticule arch, and its branch to the esophagus is divided. Umbilical tape is introduced to provide traction on the dissected esophagus, aiding the assistant in completing the dissection on the left side. The subcarinal lymph node dissection is finalized, with the assistant removing the lymph nodes, revealing the aorta, carina, and left side of the trachea.
